13.44: The following Sabbath nearly the entire city turned out to hear the word of the Lord.
13.45: When the Jewish leaders saw the crowds, they burned with jealousy. They began arguing against everything Paul said, hurling insults at him.
13.46: Paul and Barnabas gave them a direct answer: 'God's word had to be spoken to you first. But since you push it away and make yourselves judges that you are unworthy of eternal life—we are turning to the Gentiles.'
13.47: 'Because that is what the Lord commissioned us to do: "I have made you a light for the nations, so that you will bring rescue to the very ends of the earth."'
13.48: The Gentiles were overjoyed when they heard this. They celebrated and gave honour to the Lord's word. And everyone who had been set apart for eternal life came to faith.
13.49: God's word spread across the entire region.
13.50: But the Jewish leaders stirred up prominent women who were devout and the leading men of the city. They whipped up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas and drove them out of the area.
13.51: Paul and Barnabas shook the dust from their feet in protest and moved on to Iconium.
13.52: And the believers they left behind were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.

14.1: At Iconium, Paul and Barnabas entered the Jewish synagogue as they had done before. Their speaking was so compelling that a great number of both Jews and Greeks came to faith.
14.2: But those among the Jews who refused to believe worked to inflame the Gentiles' minds against the believers.
14.3: Paul and Barnabas stayed on there for a long time, speaking openly and fearlessly for the Lord. He confirmed the message of his grace by giving them the power to perform signs and wonders.
14.4: The city was split. Some took the side of the Jewish leaders; others were with the apostles.
14.5: When a plan was forming among the Gentiles and Jews together—with the approval of their leaders—to assault and stone them,
14.6: they learned of it and escaped to the Lycaonian cities of Lystra and Derbe and the surrounding countryside.
14.7: There they kept right on announcing the good news.
14.8: In Lystra there was a man who had been unable to walk since birth—he had never taken a single step.
14.9: This man was listening to Paul. Paul looked at him closely and saw that he had the faith needed to be healed.
14.10: He called out with a clear voice, 'Stand up on your feet!' The man leaped up and started walking.
14.11: The crowds saw what Paul had done and began shouting in the Lycaonian language, 'The gods have taken human form and come down to us!'
14.12: They called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, since Paul was the one doing most of the talking.
14.13: The priest of the Zeus temple located just outside the city brought bulls and flower wreaths to the city gates, intending to make sacrifices along with the crowds.
14.14: When Barnabas and Paul understood what was happening, they tore their clothes and rushed into the crowd, shouting:
14.15: 'Friends, what are you doing? We are just people—human beings like you! We're here to bring you good news: turn away from these empty things to the living God, the one who made heaven and earth and sea and everything in them.'
14.16: 'In generations past, he allowed all the nations to go their own way.'
14.17: 'And yet he was never without witnesses—he showed his goodness by sending rain from heaven and crops in season, filling your lives with food and your hearts with happiness.'
14.18: Even with these words they could barely stop the crowd from offering sacrifices to them.
14.19: Then Jews arrived from Antioch and Iconium. They turned the crowd against Paul, stoned him, and dragged his body outside the city, leaving him for dead.
14.20: But the disciples formed a circle around him, and Paul got up and walked back into the city. The next day he left for Derbe with Barnabas.
14.21: They announced the good news in Derbe and made many disciples. Then they turned back—through Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch—
14.22: strengthening the believers, encouraging them to remain firm in the faith. 'The path into God's kingdom runs through many hardships,' they told them.
14.23: They appointed elders in each community, and with prayer and fasting entrusted these believers to the Lord in whom they had placed their faith.
14.24: Travelling through Pisidia, they arrived in Pamphylia.
14.25: After proclaiming God's word in Perga, they made their way down to Attalia.
14.26: From Attalia they sailed back to Antioch—the very place where they had been sent off into the grace of God for the work they had now finished.
14.27: When they arrived, they gathered the whole community and told them everything God had accomplished through them—and how he had opened the door of faith wide to the Gentiles.
14.28: They settled in there with the disciples for a considerable time.

15.1: Some men came down from Judea to Antioch and began teaching the believers: 'You cannot be saved unless you are circumcised in accordance with the custom Moses gave us.'
15.2: This set off a fierce dispute between them and Paul and Barnabas. The community decided to send Paul and Barnabas, along with some others, up to Jerusalem to lay the question before the apostles and elders.
15.3: The community sent them off with a proper farewell. As they travelled through Phoenicia and Samaria, they reported the conversion of the Gentiles everywhere they stopped, and it brought deep joy to all the believers.
15.4: When they arrived in Jerusalem, they were warmly welcomed by the community, the apostles, and the elders. They reported everything God had done through them.
15.5: But some of the believers who had come from the Pharisees stood up and insisted: 'These Gentile converts must be circumcised and made to observe the whole law of Moses.'
15.6: The apostles and elders convened to work through this question.
15.7: After a long and heated debate, Peter rose to speak. 'Friends,' he said, 'you know that some time ago God chose from among us that through my mouth the Gentiles would hear the message of the good news and come to faith.'
15.8: 'God—who sees into every heart—showed that he accepted them by giving them the Holy Spirit, exactly as he had done for us.'
15.9: 'He made no distinction whatsoever between us and them—he cleansed their hearts through faith.'
15.10: 'So why are you testing God by placing on these new believers a burden that neither our ancestors nor we ourselves have ever been able to carry?'
15.11: 'We believe it is through the grace of the Lord Jesus that we are saved—and so are they. The same way, no different.'
15.12: The whole assembly went silent and listened as Barnabas and Paul described the signs and wonders God had done through them among the Gentiles.
15.13: When they had finished, James spoke. 'Friends,' he said, 'hear me out.'
15.14: 'Simon has just told us how God first moved among the Gentiles—choosing a people from them to bear his name.'
15.15: 'The words of the prophets line up with exactly this—as it is written:'
15.16: '"When that day comes I will return and will rebuild the fallen tent of David. I will rebuild it from its ruins and restore it—"'
15.17: '"—so that all the rest of humanity may seek the Lord—yes, all the Gentiles who are called by my name. This is the word of the Lord, who does what he said he would do."'
15.18: 'All of this has been known to God from the beginning of time.'
15.19: 'Therefore my ruling is this: we should not put obstacles in the way of Gentiles who are turning to God.'
15.20: 'Instead, we should write to them asking them to stay away from food offered to idols, from sexual immorality, from meat of strangled animals, and from blood.'
15.21: 'Moses has been proclaimed city by city across many generations—his writings are read aloud in the synagogues every Sabbath.'
15.22: The apostles and elders, together with the whole community, decided to select men from among themselves and send them to Antioch alongside Paul and Barnabas. They chose Judas Barsabbas and Silas, both respected leaders among the believers.
15.23: They carried this letter: 'From the apostles and elders, your brothers—to the Gentile believers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia. Greetings.'
15.24: 'We have heard that some people who left us—without any mandate from us—have unsettled you with their words and thrown your minds into confusion.'
15.25: 'So we met together as one and decided to send chosen delegates to you, along with our dear friends Barnabas and Paul—'
15.26: 'men who have put their lives on the line for the name of our Lord Jesus the Messiah.'
15.27: 'We are sending Judas and Silas to give you this same message in person.'
15.28: 'The Holy Spirit and all of us are in agreement that no burden should be laid on you beyond these essentials:'
15.29: 'Stay away from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality. Keep clear of these things and you will be doing well. Goodbye and God's blessing.'
15.30: The delegates were sent off and went to Antioch, gathered the whole community together, and read the letter aloud.
15.31: The people were overjoyed at the encouragement it brought.
15.32: Judas and Silas were prophets as well, and they spent a great deal of time encouraging and strengthening the believers with their words.
15.33: After staying there for a while, they were sent back with warm farewells from the believers to those who had commissioned them.
15.34: Silas, however, chose to stay on.
15.35: Paul and Barnabas also stayed in Antioch, teaching and announcing the Lord's word alongside many others.
15.36: Some time later, Paul said to Barnabas, 'Let's go back and check on the believers in all the towns where we proclaimed the Lord's word—see how they're getting on.'
15.37: Barnabas was set on taking John—the one called Mark—along with them.
15.38: Paul was firmly against it, since Mark had abandoned them in Pamphylia and hadn't seen the work through.
15.39: The disagreement was so heated that they parted ways. Barnabas took Mark and sailed to Cyprus.
15.40: Paul chose Silas instead, and the community commended them to the Lord's grace as they set out.
15.41: He travelled through Syria and Cilicia, building up and strengthening the communities.

16.1: Paul arrived at Derbe and then Lystra, where he found a disciple named Timothy. His mother was a Jewish believer, but his father was Greek.
16.2: The brothers and sisters in Lystra and Iconium spoke highly of him.
16.3: Paul wanted to take Timothy along, so he circumcised him—out of consideration for the Jewish people in that region, who all knew his father was Greek.
16.4: As they went from town to town, they delivered the decisions made by the apostles and elders in Jerusalem for everyone to follow.
16.5: The churches were strengthened in their faith and grew in numbers every day.
16.6: They traveled through the region of Phrygia and Galatia, because the Holy Spirit had prevented them from preaching in the province of Asia.
16.7: When they reached the border of Mysia, they tried to enter Bithynia—but the Spirit of Jesus would not let them.
16.8: So they bypassed Mysia and went down to Troas.
16.9: That night Paul had a vision: a man from Macedonia was standing there, pleading with him: Come over to Macedonia and help us!
16.10: As soon as Paul saw the vision, we immediately made plans to leave for Macedonia—certain that God had called us to bring the good news to those people.
16.11: We set sail from Troas and ran a straight course to Samothrace, and the next day on to Neapolis.
16.12: From there we went to Philippi—a leading city of that region of Macedonia and a Roman colony. We stayed there for several days.
16.13: On the Sabbath we went outside the city gate to the riverside, where we expected to find a place of prayer. We sat down and began talking with the women who had gathered.
16.14: One of the women listening was Lydia—a dealer in purple cloth from the city of Thyatira who worshiped God. The Lord opened her heart, and she responded to what Paul was saying.
16.15: When she and her household were baptized, she invited us home. She said: If you consider me a believer in the Lord, come and stay at my house. And she convinced us.
16.16: One day on our way to the place of prayer, we encountered a slave girl who had a spirit of divination. She made a lot of money for her owners through fortune-telling.
16.17: She kept following Paul and the rest of us, shouting: These men are servants of the Most High God—they are telling you how to be saved!
16.18: She kept this up for many days. Paul finally became so exasperated that he turned to the spirit and said: In the name of Jesus Christ, I order you to come out of her! And at that moment the spirit left.
16.19: When her owners saw that their source of income was gone, they grabbed Paul and Silas and hauled them into the marketplace to face the authorities.
16.20: They brought them before the magistrates and accused them: These men are Jews and are stirring up trouble in our city!
16.21: They are promoting customs that we Romans are not allowed to accept or practice.
16.22: The crowd joined in the attack. The magistrates ordered Paul and Silas to be stripped and beaten with rods.
16.23: After a severe beating, they were thrown into prison. The jailer was ordered to guard them carefully.
16.24: Following these orders, the jailer put them in the inner cell and clamped their feet in the stocks.
16.25: Around midnight, Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God—and the other prisoners were listening.
16.26: Suddenly a massive earthquake shook the foundations of the prison. All the doors flew open at once, and everyone's chains fell off.
16.27: The jailer woke up and saw the prison doors wide open. He drew his sword and was about to kill himself, thinking the prisoners had all escaped.
16.28: But Paul shouted: Don't do it—we're all still here!
16.29: The jailer called for lights, rushed in, and fell trembling before Paul and Silas.
16.30: He brought them out and asked: Sirs, what must I do to be saved?
16.31: They answered: Believe in the Lord Jesus—and you will be saved, along with your whole household.
16.32: Then they shared the word of the Lord with him and everyone else in his household.
16.33: Right then in the middle of the night, the jailer cleaned their wounds. And he and his entire household were baptized.
16.34: He brought them into his home and set a meal before them. He and his whole household were overjoyed—they had come to faith in God.
16.35: When morning came, the magistrates sent their officers with the order: Release those men.
16.36: The jailer passed the message to Paul: The magistrates have ordered your release. You're free to go—go in peace.
16.37: But Paul said: They beat us publicly without a trial—and we are Roman citizens! They threw us in prison, and now they want to slip us out quietly? Absolutely not. Let them come here themselves and escort us out.
16.38: The officers reported this to the magistrates, who were alarmed to learn that Paul and Silas were Roman citizens.
16.39: The magistrates came to apologize. They escorted Paul and Silas out of prison and asked them to leave the city.
16.40: When they left the prison, they went to Lydia's house, where they met with the brothers and sisters, encouraged them, and then departed.

17.1: Traveling through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they arrived at Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue.
17.2: As was his practice, Paul went to the synagogue. For three Sabbaths he reasoned with them from the Scriptures,
17.3: showing from Scripture that the Messiah had to suffer and rise from the dead. He declared: This Jesus I'm telling you about is the Messiah.
17.4: Some of the Jewish people were persuaded and joined Paul and Silas—along with a large number of devout Greeks and several prominent women.
17.5: But the other Jewish leaders, stirred by jealousy, gathered some troublemakers from the marketplace, started a riot, and stormed Jason's house looking for Paul and Silas.
17.6: When they couldn't find Paul and Silas, they dragged Jason and some other believers before the city officials, shouting: These troublemakers who have turned the world upside down have come here too!
17.7: Jason has taken them in. They all defy Caesar's decrees by claiming there is another king—Jesus.
17.8: This alarmed the crowd and the city officials.
17.9: They made Jason and the others post a security bond before releasing them.
17.10: That same night the believers sent Paul and Silas off to Berea. When they arrived, they went straight to the Jewish synagogue.
17.11: The Bereans were more open-minded than the Thessalonians—they eagerly received the message and examined the Scriptures every day to see whether what Paul said was true.
17.12: As a result, many believed—including a number of prominent Greek women and men.
17.13: But when the Jewish leaders in Thessalonica found out that Paul was preaching God's word in Berea, they came there to stir up trouble and agitate the crowds.
17.14: The believers immediately sent Paul down to the coast, while Silas and Timothy stayed behind in Berea.
17.15: Paul's escorts took him all the way to Athens, then returned with a message for Silas and Timothy to join him as quickly as possible.
17.16: While Paul was waiting in Athens, he was deeply troubled to see the city completely filled with idols.
17.17: So he debated in the synagogue with the Jews and devout Greeks, and every day in the marketplace with anyone who happened to be there.
17.18: He also debated with some Epicurean and Stoic philosophers. Some scoffed: What is this word-picker trying to say? Others said: He seems to be promoting foreign gods—because Paul was preaching about Jesus and the resurrection.
17.19: They took hold of him and brought him to the Areopagus, saying: We'd like to understand this new teaching of yours.
17.20: What you're saying sounds strange to us. We want to understand what you mean.
17.21: Now, all the Athenians and the visitors staying there loved nothing more than discussing the latest ideas.
17.22: So Paul stood up in the middle of the Areopagus and said: People of Athens, I can see that you are extremely religious in every way.
17.23: As I was walking around and looking at what you worship, I noticed an altar with this inscription: TO AN UNKNOWN GOD. What you worship without knowing who he is—that is what I'm going to tell you about.
17.24: The God who made the world and everything in it—he is Lord of heaven and earth—doesn't live in temples built by human hands.
17.25: He isn't served by human hands, as if he needed anything. He himself is the one who gives life and breath and everything else to all people.
17.26: From one person he made all the nations to inhabit the whole earth. He determined their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their territories.
17.27: He did this so that people would seek him—and perhaps feel their way toward him and find him. Though he is not far from any one of us.
17.28: For in him we live and move and exist. As some of your own poets have said: We are his children.
17.29: Since we are God's children, we shouldn't think of God as an idol made of gold, silver, or stone—something shaped by human hands and imagination.
17.30: God has looked past those earlier times of ignorance—but now he commands everyone, everywhere, to turn to him.
17.31: He has set a day when he will judge the whole world with justice—by the man he has appointed. And he has given proof of this to everyone by raising him from the dead.
17.32: When they heard about the resurrection of the dead, some sneered—but others said: We'd like to hear more about this.
17.33: With that, Paul left the Areopagus.
17.34: Some people joined Paul and believed. Among them were Dionysius, a member of the Areopagus, a woman named Damaris, and others with them.

18.1: After this, Paul left Athens and traveled to Corinth.
18.2: There he met a Jewish man named Aquila, a native of Pontus who had recently arrived from Italy with his wife Priscilla—they had left because Emperor Claudius had ordered all Jews out of Rome. Paul went to see them,
18.3: and since Paul was a tentmaker—the same trade as Aquila and Priscilla—he stayed and worked with them.
18.4: Every Sabbath he debated in the synagogue, trying to persuade both Jews and Greeks.
18.5: When Silas and Timothy arrived from Macedonia, Paul devoted himself entirely to preaching—declaring to the Jewish community that Jesus is the Messiah.
18.6: But when they opposed him with insults, he shook out his clothes in protest and said: Your blood is on your own heads—I am clear of responsibility. From now on I'm going to the Gentiles.
18.7: He left the synagogue and went next door to the home of Titius Justus, a worshiper of God.
18.8: Crispus, the synagogue leader, believed in the Lord along with his entire household. And many other Corinthians who heard Paul believed and were baptized.
18.9: One night the Lord said to Paul in a vision: Don't be afraid—keep speaking and don't fall silent.
18.10: I am with you, and no one will attack or harm you—because I have many people in this city.
18.11: Paul stayed in Corinth for a year and a half, teaching the word of God.
18.12: When Gallio became governor of Achaia, the Jewish leaders made a coordinated attack on Paul and dragged him before the court.
18.13: Their charge: This man is persuading people to worship God in ways that violate the law.
18.14: Just as Paul was about to speak, Gallio cut the accusers off: If this were a matter of crime or serious wrongdoing, I would have reason to hear your case.
18.15: But since this is about words, names, and your own religious law—settle it among yourselves. I refuse to judge such matters.
18.16: And he had them thrown out of court.
18.17: Then the crowd turned on Sosthenes, the synagogue leader, and beat him right in front of the court. But Gallio paid no attention.
18.18: Paul stayed in Corinth a while longer, then said goodbye to the brothers and sisters and sailed for Syria, with Priscilla and Aquila. Before leaving, he had his hair cut at Cenchreae, fulfilling a vow he had made.
18.19: They arrived at Ephesus, where Paul left Priscilla and Aquila. He went to the synagogue and debated with the Jewish community.
18.20: They asked him to stay longer, but he declined.
18.21: He said goodbye and told them: I will come back, God willing. Then he sailed from Ephesus.
18.22: He landed at Caesarea, went up to greet the church in Jerusalem, and then went down to Antioch.
18.23: After spending some time there, Paul set out again, traveling through the region of Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening the disciples everywhere he went.
18.24: Meanwhile, a Jewish man named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, arrived in Ephesus. He was an eloquent speaker with a thorough knowledge of the Scriptures.
18.25: He had been taught the way of the Lord and was passionate in spirit. He taught accurately about Jesus—though he only knew about John's baptism.
18.26: He began speaking boldly in the synagogue. When Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they invited him home and explained the way of God to him more fully.
18.27: When Apollos decided to go to Achaia, the brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote to the believers there to welcome him. On his arrival he was a great asset to those who had believed through God's grace.
18.28: He powerfully refuted the Jewish leaders in public, showing from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Messiah.

19.1: While Apollos was in Corinth, Paul traveled through the interior regions and arrived at Ephesus. There he found some disciples
19.2: and asked them: Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you believed? They answered: We haven't even heard that there is a Holy Spirit.
19.3: He asked: Then what baptism did you receive? They said: John's baptism.
19.4: Paul explained: John's baptism called people to turn from sin. He told them to believe in the one who was coming after him—that is, Jesus.
19.5: When they heard this, they were baptized in the name of the Lord Jesus.
19.6: When Paul laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came on them, and they spoke in tongues and prophesied.
19.7: There were about twelve of them in all.
19.8: Paul went to the synagogue and spoke boldly for three months, debating and persuading people about the kingdom of God.
19.9: But some of them grew hardened and refused to believe, publicly slandering the Way. So Paul withdrew from them, taking the disciples with him, and debated daily in the lecture hall of Tyrannus.
19.10: This continued for two years, until everyone in the province of Asia—both Jews and Greeks—had heard the word of the Lord.
19.11: God did extraordinary miracles through Paul—
19.12: so that even cloths and handkerchiefs that had touched him were brought to the sick, and they were healed and evil spirits drove out.
19.13: Some traveling Jewish exorcists tried using the name of the Lord Jesus against those who had evil spirits. They would say: I command you by the Jesus that Paul preaches!
19.14: Seven sons of a Jewish chief priest named Sceva were doing this.
19.15: One day the evil spirit answered: Jesus I know—and I know about Paul. But who are you?
19.16: Then the man controlled by the evil spirit leaped on them and overpowered them all—giving them such a beating that they ran out of the house naked and bleeding.
19.17: News of this spread quickly throughout Ephesus—both Jews and Greeks heard about it. Everyone was filled with awe, and the name of the Lord Jesus was held in great honor.
19.18: Many of the believers came forward, openly confessing the practices they had been involved in.
19.19: A large number who had practiced sorcery brought their scrolls together and burned them in public. When they calculated the value, it came to fifty thousand silver coins.
19.20: In this way the word of the Lord kept spreading with powerful effect.
19.21: After all this, Paul felt compelled by the Spirit to travel through Macedonia and Achaia and go to Jerusalem. He said: After I've been there, I must also see Rome.
19.22: He sent Timothy and Erastus ahead to Macedonia, while he stayed in the province of Asia a while longer.
19.23: About that time, a serious disturbance broke out over the Way.
19.24: A silversmith named Demetrius made silver miniature shrines of Artemis and kept the craftsmen busy with plenty of work.
19.25: He called together the craftsmen and workers in related trades and said: Men, you know that our prosperity depends on this business.
19.26: You can see and hear for yourselves that this man Paul has convinced and turned away a huge number of people—not just in Ephesus but throughout almost all of Asia—claiming that gods made by human hands aren't real gods.
19.27: There's danger that not only our business will fall apart, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be brought into contempt—and Artemis herself, worshiped throughout all of Asia and the world, will be stripped of her greatness.
19.28: When they heard this, they erupted in fury and began shouting: Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!
19.29: The whole city erupted into chaos. The crowd seized Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul's traveling companions from Macedonia, and swept them into the theater.
19.30: Paul wanted to go in to face the crowd, but the disciples wouldn't let him.
19.31: Even some of the provincial officials who were Paul's friends sent him a warning, urging him not to go into the theater.
19.32: The crowd was in complete confusion—different people shouting different things. Most didn't even know why they were there.
19.33: The Jewish community pushed Alexander forward to address the crowd. He motioned for quiet and tried to make a defense on their behalf.
19.34: But when the crowd recognized he was Jewish, they all shouted together for about two hours: Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!
19.35: The city clerk quieted the crowd and said: People of Ephesus! Who doesn't know that the city of Ephesus is the guardian of the temple of the great Artemis and of the sacred image that fell from heaven?
19.36: Since this is undeniable, you should calm down and not do anything foolish.
19.37: You've brought these men here, but they haven't robbed the temple or insulted our goddess.
19.38: If Demetrius and his fellow craftsmen have a grievance against anyone, the courts are in session and there are governors—let them file formal charges.
19.39: If you have other matters to raise, they should be settled in a lawful assembly.
19.40: As it is, we are in danger of being charged with rioting over today's events. We have no good reason to explain this uproar.
19.41: After saying this, he dismissed the crowd.

20.1: When the uproar ended, Paul called the disciples together, encouraged them, said goodbye, and set out for Macedonia.
20.2: He traveled through those regions, offering many words of encouragement to the believers, and finally arrived in Greece.
20.3: He stayed there three months. When a Jewish plot was discovered against him just as he was about to sail for Syria, he decided to return through Macedonia instead.
20.4: He was accompanied by Sopater son of Pyrrhus from Berea; Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica; Gaius from Derbe; Timothy; and Tychicus and Trophimus from the province of Asia.
20.5: These men went on ahead and waited for us at Troas.
20.6: We sailed from Philippi after the Festival of Unleavened Bread and joined them at Troas five days later, where we stayed for a week.
20.7: On the first day of the week we gathered together to break bread. Paul talked with the people and, since he planned to leave the next day, kept on speaking until midnight.
20.8: The upstairs room where we were meeting was bright with many lamps.
20.9: A young man named Eutychus was sitting in the window. As Paul talked on and on, Eutychus grew deeply asleep—and fell from the third story. When they picked him up, he was dead.
20.10: Paul went down, threw himself over the young man, and put his arms around him. Don't panic—he's alive! he said.
20.11: Paul went back upstairs, broke bread, and ate. He kept talking until dawn, then departed.
20.12: The people took the young man home alive—and were enormously relieved.
20.13: We went on ahead to the ship and sailed for Assos, planning to pick up Paul there—he had made arrangements to travel there by land.
20.14: When he met us at Assos, we took him on board and went on to Mitylene.
20.15: The next day we arrived off Chios, the day after that we crossed to Samos, and the following day we arrived at Miletus.
20.16: Paul had decided to bypass Ephesus to avoid spending time in the province of Asia—he was hurrying to reach Jerusalem, if at all possible, by the day of Pentecost.
20.17: From Miletus, Paul sent word to Ephesus for the elders of the church to meet him.
20.18: When they arrived, he said: You know how I lived among you the entire time from the first day I set foot in Asia.
20.19: I served the Lord with deep humility—often in tears—and endured the trials that came from the plots of the Jewish leaders.
20.20: I never held back anything that would help you. I taught you publicly and went from house to house.
20.21: I declared to both Jews and Greeks that they must turn to God in repentance and trust in our Lord Jesus.
20.22: And now, compelled by the Spirit, I am going to Jerusalem—not knowing what will happen to me there.
20.23: Only this: in every city the Holy Spirit warns me that chains and hardships are waiting for me.
20.24: But I don't place any value on my own life—my only goal is to finish the course and complete the mission the Lord Jesus gave me: to testify to the good news of God's grace.
20.25: I know that none of you to whom I have gone proclaiming the kingdom will ever see me again.
20.26: So I declare to you today that I am innocent of the blood of all of you.
20.27: I never held back from declaring to you the whole purpose of God.
20.28: Guard yourselves and the entire flock over which the Holy Spirit has appointed you as overseers. Shepherd God's church—which he purchased at the cost of his own blood.
20.29: I know that after I leave, savage wolves will come in among you and will not spare the flock.
20.30: Even from within your own group, men will rise up and distort the truth to draw disciples after themselves.
20.31: So stay alert! Remember that for three years, night and day, I kept warning each of you with tears.
20.32: Now I entrust you to God and to the message of his grace—which is able to build you up and give you an inheritance among all who are set apart for him.
20.33: I never wanted anyone's silver, gold, or clothing.
20.34: You know that these hands of mine worked to provide for my own needs and the needs of those who were with me.
20.35: I showed you by example that by this kind of hard work we must help those who are weak. We should remember the words of the Lord Jesus himself: It is more blessed to give than to receive.
20.36: When Paul had finished speaking, he knelt down and prayed with all of them.
20.37: Everyone wept as they embraced Paul and kissed him.
20.38: What broke their hearts most was his saying that they would never see him again. They walked him down to the ship.

21.1: After tearing ourselves away from them, we set sail and went straight to Cos. The next day we reached Rhodes, and from there went on to Patara.
21.2: We found a ship making the crossing to Phoenicia, went aboard, and set sail.
21.3: When Cyprus came into view, we left it to the south and sailed on to Syria, landing at Tyre, where the ship was to unload its cargo.
21.4: We found the believers there and stayed with them for a week. Through the Spirit, they kept urging Paul not to go to Jerusalem.
21.5: When the week was over, we set out. The whole community—wives and children included—walked us out of the city. On the beach we all knelt down and prayed.
21.6: We said our goodbyes, boarded the ship, and they went back home.
21.7: Continuing from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais, where we greeted the brothers and sisters and spent a day with them.
21.8: The next day we went to Caesarea and stayed at the home of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the Seven.
21.9: He had four unmarried daughters who had the gift of prophecy.
21.10: While we were staying there a few days, a prophet named Agabus arrived from Judea.
21.11: He came up to Paul, took his belt, and used it to tie his own hands and feet. Then he said: This is what the Holy Spirit says—the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem will bind the man who owns this belt and hand him over to the Gentiles.
21.12: When we heard this, we and the local people all begged Paul not to go to Jerusalem.
21.13: Paul answered: Why are you crying and breaking my heart? I am ready not only to be bound but even to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.
21.14: When he couldn't be talked out of it, we stopped trying and said: Let the Lord's will be done.
21.15: After this we packed our things and set out for Jerusalem.
21.16: Some of the disciples from Caesarea came with us and took us to the home of Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, where we were to stay.
21.17: When we arrived in Jerusalem, the believers welcomed us warmly.
21.18: The following day Paul and the rest of us went to meet with James, and all the elders were there.
21.19: After greeting them, Paul gave a detailed account of everything God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
21.20: When they heard it, they praised God. Then they told Paul: You can see, brother, how many tens of thousands of Jewish people have believed—and they are all deeply committed to the law.
21.21: They have been told that you teach all the Jews living among the Gentiles to abandon Moses—telling them not to circumcise their children or follow our ancestral customs.
21.22: What should we do? They will certainly hear that you've arrived.
21.23: So here's what we want you to do: We have four men who have taken a vow.
21.24: Join in their purification rites and pay for their expenses so they can have their heads shaved. Then everyone will know that what they've heard about you isn't true—that you yourself live in obedience to the law.
21.25: As for the Gentile believers, we have already written to them with our ruling: they must abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality.
21.26: The next day Paul took the men and underwent purification with them. Then he went to the temple to give notice of when the purification days would end and the offering would be made for each of them.
21.27: When the seven days were nearly over, some Jewish people from the province of Asia spotted Paul in the temple. They stirred up the whole crowd and grabbed him,
21.28: shouting: People of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, our law, and this place. And now he's even brought Greeks into the temple and defiled this holy ground!
21.29: They had seen Trophimus the Ephesian with Paul in the city and assumed Paul had brought him into the temple.
21.30: The whole city was in an uproar. People came running from every direction. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, and the gates were immediately shut behind him.
21.31: While they were trying to kill him, word reached the commander of the Roman troops that all Jerusalem was in chaos.
21.32: He immediately took soldiers and officers and rushed down to the crowd. When the mob saw the commander with his soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
21.33: The commander came up, arrested Paul, and ordered him chained with two chains. He then asked who Paul was and what he had done.
21.34: Different people in the crowd shouted different things. Unable to get at the truth through the uproar, the commander ordered Paul taken into the barracks.
21.35: When Paul reached the steps, the mob was so violent that the soldiers had to carry him.
21.36: The crowd surged behind them, shouting: Away with him!
21.37: As the soldiers were about to take him inside, Paul asked the commander: May I say something to you? The commander said: Do you speak Greek?
21.38: Aren't you the Egyptian who started that revolt not long ago and led four thousand assassins out into the desert?
21.39: Paul answered: I am a Jew—a citizen of Tarsus in Cilicia, no insignificant city. Please allow me to speak to the people.
21.40: After receiving permission, Paul stood on the steps and motioned to the crowd. When silence fell, he addressed them in Aramaic:

22.1: Friends—brothers and fathers—please hear my case.
22.2: When they heard him speaking in their own Aramaic, the crowd fell into a deeper silence. Paul went on:
22.3: I am a Jew—born in Tarsus of Cilicia but raised right here in this city. I was trained at Gamaliel's feet in the precise keeping of our ancestral law, burning with zeal for God just as you all are.
22.4: I hunted down the followers of the Way to their deaths—men and women alike—arresting them and throwing them in prison.
22.5: The high priest himself and the entire council of elders can confirm it. I received official letters from them and was on my way to Damascus to arrest any followers of the Way I could find and drag them back to Jerusalem for punishment.
22.6: As I was travelling and nearing Damascus—it was around midday—a brilliant light from heaven suddenly blazed all around me.
22.7: I fell face down to the ground and heard a voice calling out, 'Saul! Saul! Why are you persecuting me?'
22.8: 'Who are you, Lord?' I asked. The voice answered, 'I am Jesus of Nazareth—the very one you are persecuting.'
22.9: My companions saw the light, but they could not make out the words of the voice speaking to me.
22.10: 'Lord, what should I do?' The Lord told me, 'Get up and go into Damascus. There everything laid out for you will be made clear.'
22.11: The glory of that light had blinded me. My companions led me by the hand all the way into Damascus.
22.12: A man named Ananias came to visit me—a devout man, faithful to the law, and held in high esteem by all the Jews living in Damascus.
22.13: He stood beside me and said, 'Brother Saul—receive your sight!' At that instant my sight returned and I could see him.
22.14: 'The God of our ancestors has chosen you,' he said, 'to know his will, to see the Righteous One, and to hear his own voice directly.'
22.15: 'You will carry his message as a witness to all humanity—testifying to what you have personally seen and heard.'
22.16: 'So what are you waiting for? Get up, be immersed, and have your sins washed away—call on his name now!'
22.17: 'Later, when I was back in Jerusalem and praying in the temple, I fell into a trance.'
22.18: 'I saw the Lord speaking to me: "Go quickly—get out of Jerusalem. They won't receive your testimony about me."'
22.19: 'But Lord,' I said, 'they know exactly what I used to do—going from synagogue to synagogue, arresting and flogging those who put their faith in you.'
22.20: 'And when they shed the blood of Stephen, your witness, I was standing right there—giving my complete approval, holding the cloaks of his killers.'
22.21: '"Go," the Lord said, "for I am sending you far away—to the Gentiles."'
22.22: The crowd had been listening right up to that one word. Then they erupted: 'Kill him! Take this man off the face of the earth! He doesn't deserve to live!'
22.23: They were screaming, ripping off their cloaks, hurling dust into the air—
22.24: the commander had Paul hauled into the barracks. He ordered him flogged under interrogation—he wanted to know exactly what had set off the crowd.
22.25: They were already strapping Paul down for the flogging when Paul spoke to the centurion standing nearby: 'Is it legal for you to flog a Roman citizen without a trial?'
22.26: The centurion heard it and went straight to the commander. 'Do you realize what you're about to do? This man is a Roman citizen.'
22.27: The commander came himself. 'Are you really a Roman citizen?' he demanded. 'Yes,' Paul said.
22.28: 'I bought my citizenship—it cost a great deal,' the commander said. 'Mine came by birth,' Paul replied.
22.29: The men who were about to interrogate Paul stepped back immediately. The commander himself was shaken—he had chained a Roman citizen.
22.30: The following day the commander decided he needed to know the real charge against Paul. He had the chains removed, summoned the chief priests and the whole Sanhedrin, and brought Paul down to stand before them.

23.1: Paul looked the Sanhedrin full in the face and spoke: 'My brothers, I have lived before God with a completely clear conscience right up to this very moment.'
23.2: At that, the high priest Ananias ordered the men standing beside Paul to strike him across the mouth.
23.3: Paul fired back: 'God will strike you down, you whitewashed wall! You sit there to judge me by the law—and then break the very law yourself by ordering me struck?'
23.4: 'How dare you insult God's high priest!' the bystanders objected.
23.5: 'Brothers, I didn't realize he was the high priest,' Paul said. 'I know the scripture: "You must not speak evil of the ruler of your people."'
23.6: Paul saw his moment: half the council were Sadducees, the other half Pharisees. He called out to all of them: 'My brothers, I am a Pharisee—a Pharisee's son! It is the hope of the resurrection that puts me on trial here today!'
23.7: The moment he said it, an argument erupted between the Pharisees and the Sadducees, and the council split down the middle.
23.8: (The Sadducees held that there is no resurrection—no angels, no spirits at all. The Pharisees affirmed all three.)
23.9: The place erupted. Some of the Pharisee scribes jumped to their feet and argued forcefully: 'We find nothing wrong with this man. What if a spirit or an angel really did speak to him?'
23.10: The argument turned violent. The commander, fearing Paul would be literally torn apart, ordered his soldiers to rush down, snatch Paul out of the middle of them, and bring him back to the barracks.
23.11: That night the Lord appeared at Paul's side: 'Be courageous,' he said. 'You have borne witness for me in Jerusalem. You must do the same in Rome.'
23.12: At daybreak a group of Jews formed a conspiracy and took a solemn oath—they would neither eat nor drink until Paul was dead.
23.13: More than forty men bound themselves to this oath.
23.14: They went to the chief priests and elders with their scheme: 'We've sworn a solemn oath—we won't eat a thing until Paul is dead.
23.15: 'Here's what you do: you and the Sanhedrin petition the commander to bring Paul down to you, claiming you need to look into his case more carefully. We'll be waiting in ambush to kill him before he even arrives.'
23.16: Paul's nephew got wind of the ambush. He went straight to the barracks and told Paul.
23.17: Paul summoned one of the centurions. 'Take this young man to the commander,' he said. 'He has something important to tell him.'
23.18: The centurion took the young man to the commander. 'The prisoner Paul called me over and asked me to bring this young man to you,' he said. 'He has something to report.'
23.19: The commander took the young man by the hand and stepped aside with him privately. 'What is it you have to tell me?' he asked.
23.20: 'The Jewish leaders have agreed to ask you to bring Paul before the Sanhedrin tomorrow,' he said, 'under the pretense of examining his case more closely.
23.21: 'Don't comply with their request. More than forty of their men are lying in ambush for him—they've taken a solemn oath not to eat or drink until they've killed him. They're poised and waiting for your word right now.'
23.22: The commander sent the young man away with strict orders: 'Don't breathe a word to anyone that you've told me this.'
23.23: He summoned two of his centurions. 'Have two hundred infantry ready to move to Caesarea tonight at nine o'clock—plus seventy cavalry and two hundred spearmen.'
23.24: 'Get horses for Paul to ride and bring him safely to Governor Felix.'
23.25: He also sent this letter ahead:
23.26: From Claudius Lysias — To His Excellency, Governor Felix: Greetings.
23.27: 'This man was seized by Jews and was about to be killed. I intervened with my troops and got him out — I had found out he is a Roman citizen.'
23.28: 'Wanting to know the specific charge against him, I brought him before their council.'
23.29: 'The charges all turned on disputes about their own law. Nothing against him warranted death or even imprisonment.'
23.30: 'When I learned of a plot against him, I sent him to you immediately. I have instructed his accusers to lay out their case before you. Farewell.'
23.31: The soldiers followed their orders: they moved Paul out under cover of night and brought him all the way to Antipatris.
23.32: The next morning they handed Paul over to the cavalry escort and marched back to Jerusalem.
23.33: On arriving in Caesarea, they delivered the letter to the governor and presented Paul before him.
23.34: Felix read the letter and asked what province Paul came from. When he learned it was Cilicia,
23.35: he said, 'I will give you a hearing once your accusers arrive,' and ordered Paul held in custody at Herod's palace.

24.1: Five days later the high priest Ananias arrived in Caesarea with a delegation of elders and a professional advocate named Tertullus. They formally presented their charges against Paul to the governor.
24.2: Tertullus was called forward and opened his speech: 'Through you, Your Excellency Felix, we have enjoyed prolonged peace, and your wise governance has brought real improvements to this nation.'
24.3: 'We acknowledge this everywhere and in every way with the deepest gratitude, most excellent Felix.'
24.4: 'I won't take up any more of your valuable time than necessary. I simply ask you to be so gracious as to hear us out briefly.'
24.5: 'Here is what we have found: this man is a public menace—a troublemaker who has incited unrest among Jews across the entire empire. He is the ringleader of the Nazarene movement.'
24.6: 'He even attempted to defile the temple itself. That is when we arrested him.'
24.8: '—and you can determine the truth of all our charges simply by examining him yourself.'
24.9: The Jews who had come supported the case, insisting that every word was accurate.
24.10: Felix nodded to Paul to speak, and Paul replied: 'Knowing that you have served as judge over this people for many years, I make my case with confidence.'
24.11: 'You can check the facts: I arrived in Jerusalem to worship no more than twelve days ago.'
24.12: 'They did not find me arguing with anyone in the temple, or stirring up a crowd in the synagogues, or anywhere else in the city.'
24.13: 'And they cannot substantiate a single charge they are making.'
24.14: 'What I will freely admit is this: I worship the God of our ancestors following the Way—what they call a sect. I believe everything the Law teaches and everything the Prophets have written.'
24.15: 'And I hold the same hope in God that these very accusers claim to share: that both the righteous and the wicked will be raised from the dead.'
24.16: 'This is precisely why I work hard to maintain a clear conscience before God and before everyone around me—always.'
24.17: 'After several years away, I came to bring relief funds to my own people and to present offerings at the temple.'
24.18: 'That is when some Jews from the province of Asia found me in the temple—I had just completed the purification rites. There was no crowd with me, no disturbance at all.'
24.19: 'They are the ones who ought to be standing before you with their charges, if they have any grievance against me—but they are absent.'
24.20: 'Otherwise, let these men present say what offence they actually found in me when I stood before the Sanhedrin—'
24.21: '—unless it is this single declaration I made as I stood there: "I am on trial today because of the resurrection of the dead."'
24.22: Felix, who knew quite a bit about the Way, adjourned the hearing. 'Once the commander Lysias comes down,' he said, 'I will give my ruling.'
24.23: He ordered the centurion to keep Paul in custody but to give him considerable latitude—and to let his friends come and go freely to care for him.
24.24: A few days later Felix arrived with his wife Drusilla—she was Jewish. He sent for Paul and listened to him speak about trusting in Jesus the Messiah.
24.25: As Paul reasoned through the right way to live, self-mastery, and the coming day when God will judge everyone, Felix grew genuinely alarmed. 'Enough for now,' he said. 'You may go. I will send for you when the moment is right.'
24.26: He was also hoping Paul would offer him money. So he kept sending for Paul regularly and having long conversations with him.
24.27: Two years passed. Then Felix was replaced by Porcius Festus. As a parting gesture to the Jewish leaders, Felix left Paul a prisoner.
24.7: 'But the commander Lysias intervened and removed him from our custody by force—'

25.1: Three days after taking up his post, Festus travelled up from Caesarea to Jerusalem.
25.2: There the chief priests and leading Jewish officials came to him and laid out their case against Paul.
25.3: They asked Festus to do them a favour and transfer Paul to Jerusalem—they were already planning to ambush and kill him on the road.
25.4: Festus replied that Paul was being held in Caesarea and that he himself would be returning there before long.
25.5: 'So let your leading men come down with me,' he said, 'and if this man has actually done anything wrong, let them bring their accusations in the proper place.'
25.6: After eight or ten days in Jerusalem, Festus went back to Caesarea. The very next day he took his seat on the tribunal and ordered Paul to be brought before him.
25.7: When Paul appeared, the Jews who had come down from Jerusalem crowded around him and hurled charge after charge at him—serious accusations, none of which they could substantiate.
25.8: Paul simply stated his defence: 'I have committed no offence against the Jewish law, against the temple, or against Caesar—none whatsoever.'
25.9: Festus, who wanted to stay on good terms with the Jewish leaders, put the question to Paul: 'Would you be willing to go up to Jerusalem and face trial there before me on these charges?'
25.10: Paul replied: 'I am standing at the tribunal of Caesar—that is exactly where I ought to be tried. I have done nothing against the Jewish people, as you yourself know perfectly well.'
25.11: 'If I have done something that deserves death, I am not trying to escape it. But if there is nothing to any of these accusations—and there is not—then no one has the right to hand me over to please them. I appeal to Caesar!'
25.12: Festus withdrew to consult his advisers, then came back with his answer: 'You have appealed to Caesar. To Caesar you go!'
25.13: Some days later King Agrippa and Bernice arrived in Caesarea on a state visit to welcome Festus.
25.14: They were there several days, and Festus used the time to lay Paul's situation before the king. 'There's a man here,' he said, 'whom Felix left behind under arrest.'
25.15: 'When I was in Jerusalem, the chief priests and elders of the Jews presented their case against him and demanded I sentence him.'
25.16: 'I told them plainly: Romans do not hand a man over for punishment before he has had the chance to face his accusers directly and make his own defence.'
25.17: 'So when they came here with me, I wasted no time. The very next day I took my seat on the tribunal and had the man brought in.'
25.18: 'When his accusers stood up, the charges they brought were nothing like what I had anticipated.'
25.19: 'Instead they had arguments with him about their own religion—and about a man named Jesus who had died, whom Paul kept insisting was alive.'
25.20: 'Since I had no idea how to handle this kind of dispute, I asked him whether he would be prepared to go to Jerusalem and face trial there on these questions.'
25.21: 'But Paul appealed to be kept under custody for the Emperor to decide his case. So I ordered him detained until I could send him to Caesar.'
25.22: Agrippa said, 'I would actually like to hear this man.' Festus replied, 'Tomorrow you will have your chance.'
25.23: The following day there was a full ceremony. Agrippa and Bernice arrived in state and entered the auditorium with the military commanders and the city's most prominent citizens. On Festus's order, Paul was brought in.
25.24: Festus addressed them: 'King Agrippa and everyone gathered here—you see this man. Every part of the Jewish community, in Jerusalem and here in Caesarea, has been demanding his death, insisting he should not be allowed to live.'
25.25: 'But my own investigation found nothing in him that deserves death. Since he appealed to the Emperor himself, I decided to send him.'
25.26: 'The trouble is, I have nothing solid to put in a letter to the Emperor. So I have brought him before you all—and especially before you, King Agrippa—hoping that this hearing will give me something concrete to report.'
25.27: 'It would be absurd to send a prisoner to Rome without being able to state clearly what he is accused of.'

26.1: Agrippa gave Paul the floor: 'You may speak on your own behalf.' Paul raised his hand and began.
26.2: 'King Agrippa, I count myself fortunate to be standing before you today to answer these charges from the Jewish leaders.'
26.3: 'You are a man who knows the Jewish world intimately—every custom, every controversy. I am asking you simply to hear me out.'
26.4: 'Every Jew who knows anything about me knows how I lived from my earliest years—first in my homeland, then in Jerusalem.'
26.5: 'They have known me from the start. If they were willing to be honest, they would confirm that I lived as a Pharisee—following the strictest interpretation of our religion.'
26.6: 'And here I stand on trial—for nothing other than the hope that comes from God's promise to our ancestors.'
26.7: 'It is the very hope our twelve tribes reach toward as they serve God earnestly, morning and night. And yet, O King, it is because of this hope that the Jews are bringing charges against me.'
26.8: 'Why is it thought unbelievable that God can raise the dead? Why?'
26.9: 'I will be honest: I myself once believed it was my duty to fight against the name of Jesus of Nazareth in every way I could.'
26.10: 'And that is exactly what I did in Jerusalem. With authority from the chief priests, I threw many of God's people in prison. When they were sentenced to death, I voted for it.'
26.11: 'Synagogue by synagogue, I had them beaten and tried to force them to deny their faith. My rage drove me even beyond our borders—I was hunting them down in foreign cities.'
26.12: 'That is what I was doing when I set out for Damascus—armed with the authority and orders of the chief priests.'
26.13: 'At midday, O King, a light blazed down from heaven—brighter than the sun itself—surrounding me and everyone travelling with me.'
26.14: 'We all fell to the ground. Then I heard a voice in our own language—Hebrew—speaking directly to me: "Saul, Saul, why are you persecuting me? You are only hurting yourself by fighting what you cannot stop."'
26.15: 'I asked, "Who are you, Lord?" The answer came: "I am Jesus—the one you are persecuting."'
26.16: '"Get up and stand. I have appeared to you for a reason: to commission you as my servant and witness—both to what you have seen today and to what I will still reveal to you."'
26.17: '"I will keep you safe from your own people and from the Gentiles—the very people I am sending you to."'
26.18: '"—to open their eyes, to turn them from darkness toward light, and from the dominion of Satan to the living God. So they may receive forgiveness for all they have done, and a share in the inheritance that belongs to all who are set apart through faith in me."'
26.19: 'And so, King Agrippa, I did not ignore the vision from heaven.'
26.20: 'Starting in Damascus, then Jerusalem, then across all of Judea, and then out to the Gentiles—I proclaimed a single message: turn back to God, and let your actions show it is real.'
26.21: 'That is what the Jewish leaders seized me for in the temple—and that is why they tried to kill me.'
26.22: 'But God has been my help, and I am still standing. To this day I testify before everyone—important or unimportant—and I say nothing that goes beyond what Moses and the prophets predicted:'
26.23: 'that the Messiah had to suffer—and that he, as the firstfruits of the resurrection, would be the one to announce the dawn of light to Israel and to all the nations.'
26.24: That was too much for Festus. He cut Paul off mid-sentence and bellowed: 'Paul, you have lost your mind! All that study has pushed you over the edge!'
26.25: 'I am not out of my mind, most excellent Festus,' Paul replied quietly. 'Everything I am saying is sober truth.'
26.26: 'The king understands these things—that is why I am speaking so directly to him. I am quite certain none of this is news to him. It did not happen in a back room somewhere.'
26.27: 'King Agrippa—do you believe the prophets? I know you do.'
26.28: Agrippa's reply was terse: 'You almost have me convinced, Paul—you nearly make me a Christian.'
26.29: Paul said quietly: 'Short time or long, I pray to God that every person in this room—not just you—would come to be exactly what I am. Except, of course, for these chains.'
26.30: At that, the king stood up. So did the governor, Bernice, and everyone else in the room.
26.31: As they withdrew to a private room, they said to each other: 'This man has done nothing to deserve death or even imprisonment.'
26.32: Agrippa summed it up to Festus: 'He could have walked free if he hadn't appealed to Caesar.'

27.1: When it was decided that we would sail for Italy, Paul and some other prisoners were handed over to a centurion named Julius, of the Imperial Regiment.
27.2: We boarded a ship from Adramyttium that was heading for ports along the coast of Asia. We set sail, and Aristarchus—a Macedonian from Thessalonica—was with us.
27.3: The next day we landed at Sidon. Julius treated Paul kindly and allowed him to visit friends who could care for him.
27.4: Setting out again, we sailed in the shelter of Cyprus because the winds were against us.
27.5: After sailing across the open sea off the coasts of Cilicia and Pamphylia, we arrived at Myra in Lycia.
27.6: There the centurion found an Alexandrian ship bound for Italy and put us aboard.
27.7: We made slow headway for many days and had difficulty reaching Cnidus. When the wind kept us from going further, we sailed in the shelter of Crete, past Cape Salmone.
27.8: We struggled along the coast and came to a place called Fair Havens, near the city of Lasea.
27.9: We had lost so much time that sailing had become dangerous—the Day of Atonement was already past. Paul warned them,
27.10: Men, I can see that this voyage is going to end in disaster and great loss—not only to the ship and cargo, but to our own lives as well.
27.11: But the centurion trusted the pilot and the ship's owner more than Paul's warning.
27.12: Since Fair Havens was not suitable for wintering, the majority voted to sail on and try to reach Phoenix—a harbor in Crete facing southwest and northwest—and winter there.
27.13: When a gentle south wind began to blow, they thought they had their chance. They weighed anchor and sailed along the shore of Crete.
27.14: But before long, a hurricane-force wind—called the Northeaster—swept down from the island.
27.15: The ship was caught by the storm and couldn't head into it. So we gave up and let ourselves be driven.
27.16: We ran in the shelter of a small island called Cauda, where we barely managed to haul in the lifeboat.
27.17: They hoisted it on deck. Then they ran ropes under the hull to hold the ship together. Afraid of running aground on the sandbars of Syrtis, they lowered the sea anchor and let the ship drift.
27.18: We were tossed so violently by the storm that the next day they began throwing the cargo overboard.
27.19: On the third day they threw the ship's equipment overboard with their own hands.
27.20: When neither sun nor stars appeared for many days and the violent storm kept raging, we finally gave up all hope of surviving.
27.21: After no one had eaten for a long time, Paul stood up among them and said: Men, you should have listened to me and not sailed from Crete—you would have avoided all this damage and loss.
27.22: But now I urge you to keep your courage. Not one of you will lose your life—only the ship will be lost.
27.23: Last night an angel of the God I belong to and serve stood beside me
27.24: and said: Don't be afraid, Paul. You must stand before Caesar. And God has graciously given you the lives of everyone sailing with you.
27.25: So take heart, men—I trust God that it will happen exactly as he told me.
27.26: But we will run aground on some island.
27.27: On the fourteenth night we were still being driven across the Adriatic. About midnight the sailors sensed they were approaching land.
27.28: They took soundings and found the water was about forty meters deep. A little farther on they took soundings again and found it was about thirty meters.
27.29: Afraid of being dashed against rocks, they dropped four anchors from the stern and prayed for daylight.
27.30: Trying to escape, some sailors lowered the lifeboat into the sea, pretending they were going to drop anchors from the bow.
27.31: Paul said to the centurion and the soldiers: Unless these men stay on the ship, you cannot be saved.
27.32: So the soldiers cut the ropes of the lifeboat and let it fall away.
27.33: Just before dawn, Paul urged everyone to eat. He said: For fourteen days you have been in suspense, going without food. Now I urge you to eat something.
27.34: You need it to survive—not one of you will lose a single hair from your head.
27.35: After saying this, he took some bread, gave thanks to God in front of everyone, broke it, and began to eat.
27.36: Everyone was encouraged—and they all ate.
27.37: In all, there were 276 people on the ship.
27.38: When they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship by throwing the grain into the sea.
27.39: When daylight came, they didn't recognize the land. But they saw a bay with a beach and decided to try to run the ship aground there.
27.40: They cut the anchors and left them in the sea. They loosened the ropes that held the rudders, raised the foresail to catch the wind, and headed for the beach.
27.41: But the ship hit a sandbar and ran aground. The bow stuck fast and couldn't move, while the stern was broken to pieces by the pounding waves.
27.42: The soldiers planned to kill the prisoners to prevent any from swimming away and escaping.
27.43: But the centurion, wanting to save Paul, stopped them. He ordered those who could swim to jump in first and get to land.
27.44: The others were to follow, some on planks and some on wreckage from the ship. And so everyone made it safely to land.

28.1: Once we were safely ashore, we learned the island was called Malta.
28.2: The islanders showed us remarkable kindness. They built a fire and welcomed all of us—it was raining and cold.
28.3: Paul gathered a bundle of brushwood. As he put it on the fire, the heat drove out a viper that latched onto his hand.
28.4: When the islanders saw the snake hanging from his hand, they said to each other: This man must be a murderer. Even though he escaped the sea, Justice won't let him live.
28.5: But Paul shook the snake off into the fire and was completely unharmed.
28.6: The people expected him to swell up or suddenly drop dead. But after waiting a long time and seeing nothing unusual happen, they changed their minds and said he was a god.
28.7: The nearby estate belonged to Publius, the chief official of the island. He welcomed us and generously hosted us for three days.
28.8: His father was in bed, sick with fever and dysentery. Paul went to him, prayed, placed his hands on him, and healed him.
28.9: When this became known, the rest of the sick people on the island came to Paul and were healed.
28.10: They honored us in many ways. When we were ready to sail, they provided everything we needed.
28.11: After three months we sailed in an Alexandrian ship that had wintered there—its figurehead was the twin gods Castor and Pollux.
28.12: We put in at Syracuse and stayed three days.
28.13: From there we sailed around and reached Rhegium. A south wind came up the next day, and the day after we arrived at Puteoli.
28.14: There we found believers who invited us to spend a week with them. And so we came to Rome.
28.15: The brothers and sisters there had heard we were coming and traveled as far as the Forum of Appius and Three Taverns to meet us. When Paul saw them, he thanked God and took courage.
28.16: When we arrived in Rome, Paul was allowed to live by himself, with a soldier assigned to guard him.
28.17: Three days after arriving, Paul called together the Jewish leaders of Rome. When they gathered, he said: Brothers, I have done nothing against our people or the customs of our ancestors. Yet I was arrested in Jerusalem and handed over to the Romans.
28.18: After examining me, they wanted to release me—I was not guilty of anything deserving death.
28.19: But when the Jewish leaders objected, I was forced to appeal to Caesar—not because I had any charge to bring against my own nation.
28.20: That is why I asked to see you and talk with you. I am bound with this chain because of the hope of Israel.
28.21: They said: We have not received any letters from Judea about you, and none of the brothers who have come from there has reported or said anything bad about you.
28.22: But we'd like to hear directly from you what you believe. We do know that this sect is spoken against everywhere.
28.23: They set a day to meet with him, and a large crowd came to where Paul was staying. From morning until evening he explained everything—testifying about the kingdom of God and persuading them about Jesus from both Moses and the Prophets.
28.24: Some were convinced by what he said, but others refused to believe.
28.25: They disagreed with one another and began to leave. But Paul had a final word: The Holy Spirit was right when he spoke to your ancestors through Isaiah the prophet:
28.26: Go to this people and say: You will hear and hear but never understand. You will look and look but never see.
28.27: For the hearts of this people have grown callous—their ears are hard of hearing and their eyes are closed. Otherwise they might see with their eyes, hear with their ears, understand with their hearts—and turn to me, and I would heal them.
28.28: Therefore I want you to know that God's salvation has been sent to the Gentiles—and they will listen!
28.30: For two whole years Paul stayed in his own rented house and welcomed everyone who came to visit him.
28.31: He proclaimed the kingdom of God and taught about the Lord Jesus Christ with complete freedom—and no one stopped him.
28.29: After Paul said this, the Jews left, arguing heatedly among themselves.
